description: Rules for venturing into the unknown: structured procedures for sites and wilderness, rewards for discovery, and the maps that record it.

site.description: Rules for exploring bounded adventure sites such as dungeons, ruins, and complexes.

focuses.dungeon_turn_procedure: Site exploration runs on fixed dungeon turns of roughly ten minutes each, during which the party moves and acts while the GM checks light, encounters, and time (as in Old-School Essentials or Shadowdark).
focuses.room_stocking: Rooms are keyed using stocking tables that set ratios of monsters, treasure, traps, and empty space (as in B/X dungeon stocking).
focuses.restocking_dungeons: Cleared areas repopulate over in-game time, so revisited sites present new inhabitants and altered power balances.
focuses.megadungeon_factions: A large site is inhabited by rival factions with goals and territories, letting players ally with, trick, or play them against one another.
focuses.zone_exploration: Sites are broken into zones or areas explored as narrative units rather than mapped room by room, trading granularity for pace.
focuses.dungeon_level_depth: Danger and reward scale with dungeon level, so stairways down are meaningful commitments (the megadungeon depth convention of early D&D).
focuses.random_dungeon_generation: Corridors, rooms, and contents are generated with tables or tiles during play, so no one knows the layout in advance (as in Four Against Darkness or the AD&D DMG's random dungeon tables).
focuses.keyed_locations: The site is prepared as a map with a numbered key describing each area's contents, revealed as players arrive.
focuses.room_searching: Searching rooms and features is a structured action that costs time and offers codified find chances, making thoroughness a gamble against the clock.
focuses.stuck_doors: Doors may be stuck or locked, requiring force-open rolls, tools, or spikes, and listening at them is its own procedure (as in B/X).
focuses.dungeon_dressing: Tables of sensory details, debris, and oddities dress areas with non-mechanical texture that rewards player attention.
focuses.lock_and_key_gating: Progress through a site is gated by keys, levers, and switches found elsewhere, structuring exploration into loops and backtracking.

wilderness.description: Rules for exploring open country: terrain, weather, navigation, and journeys off the map's edge.

focuses.hexcrawl_procedure: Overland exploration runs as a per-hex procedure of moving, checking for encounters, and discovering contents, turning the map into discrete explorable cells (as in classic hexcrawl campaigns).
focuses.landmark_navigation: Travelers orient by visible landmarks rather than coordinates, so what can be seen from where becomes navigational information.
focuses.terrain_types: Regions are typed as forest, swamp, mountain, and so on, with type driving speed, encounters, forage, and visibility.
focuses.weather_fronts: Weather systems are tracked as moving fronts that affect regions differently as they pass, rather than as one global condition.
focuses.region_generation: Procedures generate regions with terrain layouts, sites, and lairs to fill maps ahead of or during play (as in the sandbox generators of Worlds Without Number).
focuses.points_of_interest: Notable sites are seeded across the map to reward off-road exploration and give distant travel visible goals.
focuses.depthcrawl: Journeys into an otherworldly expanse are tracked by abstract depth rather than geography, with deeper results growing stranger (as in The Gardens of Ynn or The Stygian Library).
focuses.journey_roles: Each traveler takes a role such as guide, scout, hunter, or lookout, with role-specific tests deciding the journey's fortunes (as in The One Ring or Forbidden Lands).
focuses.crossings_obstacles: Rivers, cliffs, and passes are obstacles demanding checks, equipment, or detours, giving terrain tactical teeth.
focuses.seafaring_exploration: Charting waters brings distinct rules for wind, currents, provisioning, and the hazards of sailing beyond the known map.
focuses.overland_sightlines: Rules define how far characters can see across terrain, letting towers, hills, and smoke reveal distant hexes or landmarks.

discovery.description: Rules for finding what is hidden and the rewards that make discovery worthwhile.

focuses.fog_of_war: Unexplored areas are hidden from players until revealed by presence or scouting, keeping the map itself a reward (as in hidden board sections or virtual tabletop fog).
focuses.map_revealing: Portions of a pre-made map are revealed as they are reached, by turning atlas pages or uncovering panels, so the world is fixed but unknown (as in Sleeping Gods).
focuses.secret_doors: Hidden passages and compartments can be found through deliberate search procedures or passive detection chances, rewarding methodical suspicion (as in B/X secret door rolls).
focuses.treasure_maps: Maps found as treasure point to caches or sites elsewhere, chaining one discovery into the next (a classic D&D treasure type).
focuses.exploration_xp: Characters earn advancement for discovering new places such as sites, hexes, or wonders, making exploration itself the reward loop (as in Forbidden Lands).
focuses.rumor_tables: Rumor tables seed hooks and half-truths in settlements, pointing expeditions toward, or astray from, the map's contents.
focuses.scouting: A scout may range ahead to preview dangers or contents before the group commits, trading time and separation risk for information.
focuses.discovery_tiles: Face-down tokens on the map yield rewards or hazards when first reached, spicing expansion with gambles (as in Eclipse's discovery tiles).
focuses.unlocking_content: Sealed envelopes, boxes, or card packs open when conditions are met, permanently adding new rules and components (as in Charterstone's crates).
focuses.first_visit_triggers: Locations fire scripted events the first time they are entered, making initial discovery a distinct experience from revisits (as in The 7th Continent).
focuses.hidden_caches: Caches are concealed within described scenes and found only by interacting with the right feature, rewarding attentive play over dice.

procedures.description: Timing and pressure procedures that pace exploration and generate danger.

focuses.overloaded_encounter_die: A single per-turn die roll resolves multiple time pressures at once, covering encounters, signs, light, and fatigue in one throw (the OSR overloaded encounter die).
focuses.tension_pool: Dice accumulate in a visible pool as time passes or the party makes noise, and rolling the pool resolves whether danger materializes (the tension pool technique).
focuses.torchlight_deadlines: Expiring light acts as the expedition's pacing clock, forcing depth-versus-retreat decisions as flames burn down (as in Shadowdark).
focuses.wandering_monster_checks: Periodic dice checks determine whether wandering monsters appear, taxing time spent and rewarding decisiveness (as in B/X's one-in-six checks).
focuses.reaction_rolls: When creatures are encountered, a reaction roll sets their starting disposition from hostile to friendly, so not every encounter is a fight (as in B/X's 2d6 reactions).
focuses.encounter_distance: The distance at which an encounter begins is rolled based on terrain and surprise, shaping whether parley, flight, or ambush is possible.
focuses.surprise_checks: A check determines whether either side is surprised, granting the alert party a free round or an escape window.
focuses.noise_attraction: Loud actions trigger noise checks that draw threats toward the source, making stealth and restraint survival tools (as in Nemesis's noise rolls).
focuses.rest_turns: Parties must rest periodically during exploration, classically one turn in six, or suffer fatigue penalties, building pacing into the procedure (as in B/X).

mapping.description: Rules for how the explored world is represented, assembled, and permanently recorded.

focuses.player_cartography: Players draw their own map from the GM's descriptions, and their errors, gaps, and guesses become part of play.
focuses.tile_reveal: The map is built by drawing and placing tiles as exploration proceeds, so the layout emerges during play (as in Betrayal at House on the Hill).
focuses.modular_boards: The board is assembled from modular sections that vary each session, so familiar rules meet fresh geography (as in Catan or Twilight Imperium).
focuses.legacy_map_stickers: Permanent stickers and markings alter the shared map across a campaign, accumulating a physical history of past sessions (as in Pandemic Legacy or Charterstone).
focuses.location_card_maps: Sites are laid out as connected location cards with movement between them, abstracting geography into a card network (as in Arkham Horror: The Card Game).
focuses.shifting_map_tiles: Map segments slide or rotate during play, changing routes and separating explorers (as in Labyrinth or Room 25).
